# Comparing Regional Qualifications Initiatives Around the Globe

## EXPLORING THE FEASIBILITY OF COMPARISON WITH THE EQF AND CREATING A GLOBAL DIALOGUE FOR COOPERATION

## Breadcrumb

### About

The ETF study Regional Qualifications Initiatives around the Globe 2020, was developed by ETF to map the latest developments of Regional Qualifications Frameworks (RQFs) and explore the feasibility of comparison with the EQF (European Qualifications Framework).

The study was undertaken in the framework of the work of the project group on Comparison of the EQF with other RQFs and NQFs (National Qualifications Frameworks). While there is a lot of literature on NQFs, the literature on RQFs is much more limited, and there was a need to get a better overview of the RQF initiatives in the world in order to understand the potential for comparison with the EQF.

The study showed that there are more RQF initiatives existing than were known before, but many of them are still in an early stage of development. RQFs share common features and challenges but are also unique in many ways. Much of the information on RQFs was obtained through interviews as only limited information is available online. The COVID crisis has changed the way RQFs were perceived and functioned.

The interviews that were organized with representatives of the RQF initiatives clearly showed a strong interest in the findings of the study and in collaboration and dialogue between RQFs and with EQF. The webinar is therefore an opportunity to discuss the role of RQFs and exchange views on how to support dialogue and cooperation, beyond the comparison with the EQF.

Participants will be from the RQF initiatives, the EQF AG (Advisory Group), European Commission DG EMPL, Cedefop and ETF, the WRLs Group organized by Unesco and organisations supporting the development of RQFs.

In order to bridge the time difference between global participants the event is organized in two parts: A discussion on the **morning** of **19 May** with participants from Europe, Africa, Asia and the Pacific. This discussion will have translation in **English and French**.   
A second session and discussion is planned for the **afternoon** of **19 May** for participants from Europe, the Caribbean and Latin America and the Commonwealth of learning Secretariate. This discussion will be in **English and Spanish**.

The workshop is part of ETF’s work on the internationalization of qualification systems and will feed into its support for the maximalization of the currency of qualifications and international transparency.

ETF will follow up with conclusions from the discussion and is committed to develop a dedicated page on RQFs on ETF [Open Space](https://openspace.etf.europa.eu/resources/regional-qualifications-frameworks-around-globe-2020) (please sign in to access) to facilitate the exchange of information and dialogue.

### nqf 7 vs rqf 7

### Benefits of studying nqf 7 vs rqf 7

When comparing NQF 7 and RQF 7 qualifications, it's essential to understand the differences in their frameworks and how they can impact your career prospects. NQF 7 qualifications are part of the National Qualifications Framework, while RQF 7 qualifications are part of the Regulated Qualifications Framework. Both frameworks are recognized in the UK, but NQF 7 qualifications are more widely accepted internationally. Choosing the right qualification can make a significant difference in your career advancement and opportunities for further education. It's crucial to research and consider the specific requirements and benefits of each framework to make an informed decision that aligns with your goals.   
  
By understanding the distinctions between NQF 7 and RQF 7 qualifications, you can make a strategic choice that will enhance your professional development and open doors to new possibilities. Stay informed and empowered to take your career to the next level with the right qualification.



### Career path

| Career Role | Estimated Salary (£) |
| --- | --- |
| NQF 7 vs RQF 7 Analyst | £30,000 - £50,000 |
| NQF 7 vs RQF 7 Consultant | £40,000 - £70,000 |
| NQF 7 vs RQF 7 Manager | £50,000 - £80,000 |
| NQF 7 vs RQF 7 Researcher | £35,000 - £60,000 |



### Learn keyfacts about nqf 7 vs rqf 7

NQF 7 and RQF 7 are both qualifications frameworks used in the UK to classify the level of education and qualifications. NQF 7 refers to the National Qualifications Framework, while RQF 7 stands for the Regulated Qualifications Framework. Both frameworks are equivalent to a Master's degree level, indicating a high level of academic achievement. However, the RQF is the newer framework introduced in 2015 to replace the NQF and is designed to be more flexible and responsive to the needs of learners and employers. It is important to note that qualifications from both frameworks are recognized and respected by universities and employers in the UK and internationally.



### Who is nqf 7 vs rqf 7 for?

| Target Audience | Percentage |
| --- | --- |
| Higher Education Administrators | 30% |
| Educational Consultants | 20% |
| Policy Makers in Education | 15% |
| Academic Advisors | 10% |
| Students Pursuing Higher Education | 25% |

The course 'nqf 7 vs rqf 7' is designed for a diverse range of professionals and individuals involved in the field of education. The target audience for this course includes:
- Higher Education Administrators (30%): Professionals responsible for overseeing and managing academic programs and policies at universities and colleges.
- Educational Consultants (20%): Experts who provide guidance and support to educational institutions on curriculum development and accreditation processes.
- Policy Makers in Education (15%): Individuals involved in shaping and implementing education policies at local, national, or international levels.
- Academic Advisors (10%): Professionals who offer guidance and support to students in making informed decisions about their academic and career paths.
- Students Pursuing Higher Education (25%): Individuals currently enrolled in or planning to enroll in higher education programs seeking a better understanding of the differences between nqf 7 and rqf 7 qualifications.
This course aims to provide valuable insights and knowledge to help these target audiences navigate the complexities of the nqf 7 and rqf 7 frameworks in the education sector.

--- SOURCE 4: Comparing regional qualifications framework initiatives ... ---

SUMMARY:
RQFs and NQFs will need to be part of this transition out of the pandemic era and into one that involves digital, green and social

FULL CONTENT:
![ETF logo](/themes/contrib/etf_foundation/logo.svg "ETF Home")

## Select site language

## Main navigation

## Breadcrumb

![webinar on qualifications](/sites/default/files/styles/xx_large/public/2021-05/microsoftteams-image_17.png?h=4c07d534&itok=JYIKid1k)

# Comparing regional qualifications framework initiatives around the globe

Seventeen Regional Qualification Frameworks (RQFs) initiatives exist at some stage of development around the world. Based on a recently published study by the European Training Foundation, the people running these initiatives want to collaborate and exchange information with their peers worldwide. A pilot project linking the 27-country European Union (EU) with the 16-country Southern African Development Community (SADC) has been launched. Other similar endeavours are envisioned.

Those were the main takeaways from two online interactive sessions on 19 May called “Comparing regional qualification initiatives around the globe,” sponsored by the ETF. One with French translation, the other with Spanish, the two events included dozens of participants from around the globe.

The European Qualification Framework (EQF) largely informed the work of researchers as they learned about other programmes, but it is considered just one among a wide range of initiatives.

“Comparison means treating qualifications frameworks as equal partners,” said Koen Nomden, team leader of the DG EMPL at the European Commission. “Dialogue and mutual respect are keys to any comparison exercise.”

Speakers provided short overviews of all of the leading RQFs around the world, and then highlighted some common threads. As outlined by Monika Auzinger of 3S, main issues include:

· “Ownership” of the initiative – This is linked to the level of commitment of the actors. Often these efforts involve international donors. Most initiatives are linked to regional economic integration policies.

· What they want to achieve – Many, for example, aim to promote the mobility of workers and learners by making sure that their diplomas, certificates and other evidence of learning are accepted in all member states; transparency and comparability of qualifications are key, moving towards increased harmonization

· The importance of the goals of the relevant regional intergovernmental organization

· The scope of the programme, e.g., whether on higher education, vocational education and training, lifelong learning, a combination, etc.

Most RQFs find themselves in a developmental stage. Of the 17, only three are considered to be “fully operational,” meaning that they are in line with the all of the relevant national qualifications frameworks (NQFs), according to Arjen Deij of the ETF.  He outlined a series of key factors that he called the Five Cs:

· COVID-19 – the pandemic has had a huge impact on all of the countries that are involved in RQFs, most of which are low and middle income

· Common Qualifications – among some nations of the Caribbean, for example, a framework pre-dates the existence of a formal RQF body, thereby setting an interesting precedent

· Climate Change and Green Growth – a changing world requires new skills

· Catalogues and Databases

· Common Guidelines

As RQFs evolve, notably in Africa, there are three possible scenarios, said Eduarda Castel Branco of the ETF. The first would establish connections, with peer learning, capacity development and the creation of shared tools. A further step would involve the establishment of mutual trust, which would involve things such as closer networking, the alignment of qualifications, and the recognition of prior learning. A higher stage, which she called “new horizons,” would lead to the widespread acceptance of an agreed-upon qualifications framework with automatic mutual recognition and, in the case of Africa, growing international acceptance of qualifications awarded on the continent.

In breakout groups, participants discussed two issues: (1) the most pressing needs for RQFs that can be addressed by international cooperation, and (2) how to establish and improve such collaboration. Castel Branco summed up some of the main proposals that emerged from the discussions.

In terms of issues that can be at least partially addressed by international cooperation, she listed:

· sharing experiences and tools among RQFs

· studying the different referencing and alignment methods and processes

· financing, with a nod to an Erasmus initiative that is on the horizon

· the promotion of interactions among RQFs

· efforts to establish mutual trust, which Castel Branco called “probably the outcome of all these interactions and cooperation”

· working together on learning outcomes approaches

· the need for communication and awareness raising

As for ways to establish and improve cooperation, she outlined the following elements:

· jointly developing qualifications

· sharing experiences about best practices

· creating stronger links among experts in different regions

· promoting peer learning

Castel Branco called attention to a handful of other issues that emerged from the discussions. They included:

· the need to ensure “fair mobility,” which will require a common language about qualifications

· the desire to avoid repetition internationally, which would imply that RQFs around the world recognize each other

· the need to learn from the experiences of RQFs and NQFs around the world

“For more than a year, we have been going together through this unprecedented crisis of COVID,” said Castel Branco. “We now have this incredible opportunity to engage in a recovery process and to build on the lessons learned” – especially in terms of digitalization and online learning.

Some analysts believe that half of the workforce will need to develop new skills in the next five years, she said. RQFs and NQFs will need to be part of this transition out of the pandemic era and into one that involves digital, green and social transformations. Echoing what many people are saying, she urged that the focus should be on “building back better.”

# What are the Differences between NVQ, QCF, RQF framework?

The UK’s **[qualification framework](https://www.gov.uk/search/all?keywords=qualification+framework+United+kingdom&order=relevance)**serves as the overarching structure encompassing all regulated qualifications in the country. NVQ, QCF, and RQF represent different iterations of this framework, each aiming to improve upon the previous version in terms of clarity, flexibility, and quality assurance. They all ensure that qualifications meet rigorous standards and provide effective pathways to employment or further education. The acronyms NVQ, QCF, and RQF can be a confusing maze for students attempting to navigate the UK educational system. These frameworks, which are meant to direct establishing decisions, frequently leave students entangled in a maze of ambiguity.

For students, this lack of guidance and assistance can result in several issues, such as choosing the incorrect qualification, which can be expensive, or choosing a framework incompatible with one’s learning style or professional objectives. Navigating the incorrect framework can cause time and effort to be lost, impeding students’ progress toward their learning objectives. Demoralization and frustration can lead to feeling discouraged and less motivated due to the uncertainty and lack of support.

Still Not Understanding the UK Qualification Standards: Let’s revisit history to see how NVQ transitioned to RQF.

**NVQ TO QCF**

To provide a framework for qualifications centered on training and development, NVQs were introduced in the 1980s. By 2008, the method was outdated, and learners could now advance and move up the professional ladder more quickly, thanks to the QCF. NVQs were eventually incorporated into the QCF framework. All NVQs were moved into the QCF officially in 2011, allowing all degrees earned in the UK to have the same credits and achievement levels.

**QCF TO RQF**

Reviewing the**[QCF](https://www.twinemployment.com/uk/blog/what-is-qcf-the-new-nvq/)** was necessary as it was adopted as the primary framework for UK vocational courses. The results demonstrated that the QCF framework gave too much weight to structure at the expense of validity. [**RQF**](https://ofqual.blog.gov.uk/2015/10/01/explaining-the-rqf/) qualifications were introduced in response to this. The choice was made in October 2015 to switch from the QCF to the RQF. As a result of the RQF eliminating the strict deadlines related to certification sizes, students could now study from home at their own pace. The QCF credentials were completely replaced with the RQF Framework in 2018.

Throughout these transitions, the role of regulatory institutions cannot be overlooked. The National Council for Vocational Qualifications (NCVQ) was the first regulatory body established in 1986 to reform and rationalize the system of vocational qualifications by establishing a national vocational qualification framework; the NCVQ introduced and endorsed competence‐based qualifications which were awarded by vocational awarding bodies such as the City and Guilds of London Institute. In 1997, it merged with [t](https://www.oxfordreference.com/display/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100224520)he School Curriculum and Assessment Authority (SCAA) to form the Qualifications and Curriculum Authority (QCA).

This represented the policy direction at that time, which was to create stronger links between general and vocational education. Fast forward to subsequent developments, the**[QCA](https://www.oxfordreference.com/display/10.1093/oi/authority.20110803100357405)** underwent another transition. In 2010, the QCA was replaced by the Office of Qualifications and Examinations Regulation (Ofqual). This transition from QCA to Ofqual aimed to further enhance the UK’s regulatory framework for qualifications and examinations. The establishment of Ofqual signified a renewed focus on maintaining standards, ensuring fairness, and building trust in the education and assessment system.

Therefore, the trajectory from NCVQ’s inception in 1986, its merger with SCAA in 1997 to form QCA, and finally, the transition from QCA to Ofqual in 2010 reflects the continuous efforts to refine and adapt the educational landscape, creating a more cohesive and effective system for both vocational and general education. With its inception in 2010, Ofqual became the guardian of standards in education, playing a critical role in maintaining the integrity of assessments and examinations. It continued the commitment to aligning education with industry needs, echoing the initial objectives set by NCVQ. Now let’s explore Ofqual: what it is, how it works, and which functions are most crucial.

**[OFQUAL](https://www.accreditedqualifications.org.uk/office-of-qualifications-and-examinations-regulation-ofqual.html)**

Having replaced the Qualifications and Curriculum Authority (QCA) as England’s independent regulator, the Office of Qualifications and Examinations Regulation (Ofqual) was established in 2010. This shift was a turning point in the development of UK educational oversight. To protect the integrity and standards of educational qualifications, Ofqual plays a crucial function its important roles include:

***Establishing Standards***: Ofqual is tasked with creating and maintaining strong standards for qualifications in education, ensuring that they correspond with the required knowledge and abilities

***Quality Assurance***: To ensure the validity and caliber of evaluations, the regulator actively supervises the creation and management of qualifications, certifies awarding bodies, and conducts reviews.

***Fairness and Accessibility***: In addition to addressing issues of inclusion, making appropriate accommodations, and guarding against malpractice, Ofqual constantly works to guarantee that assessments are fair and available to all students.

***Regulatory Compliance***: Under strict oversight, Ofqual ensures that awarding organizations follow all applicable regulations. The regulator looks at complaints and makes the required corrections.

***Public Confidence***: By maintaining high standards, Ofqual significantly contributes to the public’s trust in the educational system. This gives employers, instructors, parents, and students confidence regarding the validity and dependability of academic credentials.

The Office of Qualifications and Examinations Regulation (OFQUAL) continues to play a crucial role in overseeing and regulating qualifications to ensure standards and quality are maintained. OFQUAL’s activities include quality assurance, accreditation of awarding organizations, setting and maintaining standards, and contributing to developing qualifications-related policies.

In summary, the history of the qualifications framework in the UK reflects a commitment to adapt and improve, addressing the evolving needs of learners and the workforce while maintaining high standards and ensuring the credibility of qualifications. Let us dive into details of what NVQ, QCF and RQF are.

## **What Are NVQs?**

NVQs, an acronym often called**[National Vocational Qualifications](https://www.businessballs.com/careers-advice/nvqs-national-vocational-qualifications/#:~:text=NVQ%20development,levels%20and%2011%20occupational%20areas/)**, are work-based credentials such as childcare, teaching assistant, electrician, etc., designed to evaluate your capacity to finish a task to a predetermined standard. The NVQ has been around since 1986 and has been a mainstay of British educational frameworks. NVQ courses have taken on many different forms over the years, with the regulatory bodies changing, the grading criteria and the list of subjects offered as well. From entry-level to postgraduate, NVQs are offered at different levels and can be completed in a variety of industries. A series of observational studies evaluate them, and a portfolio that includes any work you have completed that exemplifies the knowledge and abilities you have gained. Let’s probe into the degrees of the NVQ framework to gain a detailed comprehension of its structure.

The National Vocational Qualifications (NVQ) Framework is a seven-level qualification framework. A Qualification is generally a package of competency units to suit requirements in a particular position in the labour market.

The criteria for giving levels to qualifications are based on three parameters.

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **NVQ Level** | **Qualification Category** | **Description** |
| Level  1 | National Certificate | Recognizes the acquisition of a core of entry-level competencies |
| Level  2 Level  3Level  4 | National Certificate | Recognizes increasing levels of competencies. Level 4 qualification provides for all national craftsmanship. |
| Level  5Level  6 | National DiplomaHigher National Diploma | Recognizes increasing level of competencies  ranging from technician level to management level |
| Level 7 | Bachelor’s Degree or equivalent | This level includes planning , re-sourcing & management process |

 National Vocational Qualifications allow students to gain the skills they need for these jobs while working or studying. An NVQ denotes a specific level of competence that employers can use to gauge a candidate’s suitability for a role. An NVQ is ideal for anyone who knows their desired job and needs to gain qualifications to secure the position.

**What has**[**replaced**](https://www.ymcaawards.co.uk/faqs/will-term-nvq-be-removed)**NVQ?**

National Vocational Qualifications (NVQs) were developed in the UK in the 1980s to offer practical training comparable to academic degrees. NVQs were intended to provide students with the abilities and information required to be successful in their chosen fields. They were offered in a variety of areas, from engineering to courses in beauty therapy. Nevertheless, they were progressively superseded until 2015 by other qualifications, such as the QCF. The regulatory framework supporting NVQs was withdrawn in 2015 and replaced by the Regulated Qualifications Framework (RQF), although the term “NVQ” may be used in RQF qualifications if they “are based on recognized occupational standards, work-based and/or simulated work-based assessment, and where they confer occupational competence”.

*Note: NVQs are equivalent to SQVs offered in Scotland and are recognized throughout England, Wales, and Northern Ireland.*

## **What Is QCF?**

The **[Qualifications and Credit Framework (QCF)](https://www.twinemployment.com/uk/blog/what-is-qcf-the-new-nvq/)** takes all qualifications and allocates them a credit value. This widely used framework for adult learning is intended to make securing and utilizing your qualifications more uncomplicated and more straightforward than ever before. The new QCF (Qualification and Credit Frame) has replaced the NVQ courses

**The breakdown of the QCF mark system is as follows:**

To begin, you must accumulate credits, which require roughly 10 hours of study time. Units are then earned by accumulating credits, with various courses requiring varying amounts of credits to advance. Then, units progress toward qualifications.

There are three different awards you can achieve when completing a QCF. To achieve an ‘Award’ level QCF, you need 1-12 credits; this is the lowest qualification you can achieve. Next is the ‘Certificate’ level QCF, which you need between 13 and 36 credits to achieve; this is the second highest **[qualification](https://inspirelondoncollege.co.uk/ofqual-courses/)** you can get. Lastly is a ‘Diploma’ level QCF, which you must complete over 37 credits to earn. It doesn’t matter which qualification you take or achieve; the quality of the work will then be marked from the entry-level 1 going up to level 8 to give your work a suitable mark for employers to look at.

With the implementation of QCF, qualifications are now more flexible and straightforward to fulfill. The primary benefit of the QCF is that it allows individuals to pursue a qualification while working a full-time job! For those who are in that circumstance, this is quite helpful.  Additionally, it is particularly beneficial for jobless individuals because it allows them to search for jobs and attend interviews anytime, increasing their chances of landing a position.

A further advantage of the new unit system on a QFC is that credits completed for one unit can be applied against subsequent units you are pursuing. Thus, once you have completed a unit, it is finished. No matter how many credits are needed for that particular unit. This implies that you won’t waste time repeatedly learning the same material. As you can see, QCF exists to facilitate your job search and help businesses discover how you can contribute significantly. You will have a far better grasp of your goals, how to reach them, and who you can accomplish them with as a result, which will prevent you from being stuck in a position where there is no possibility of advancement.

*Note: (As of October 2015, RQF (Regulated Qualifications Framework) replaced QCF (Qualifications and Credit Framework). As of 30th September 2015, all awarding organizations were expected to begin removing QCF from their qualification titles. This should have been completed by the end of 2017.*

## **What Is RQF? (The New QCF?)**

In October 2015, the Qualifications and Credit Framework (QCF) was replaced by the [**Regulated Qualifications Framework (RQF)**](https://las.ac/the-regulated-qualification-framework-rqf/)to facilitate easier comparison and interpretation of the various qualifications. Regulated Qualifications Framework is designed to let you index qualifications based on their level and size. This means the higher the qualification, the greater the complexity and difficulty of skills/knowledge associated with it.

RQFs are in place because they provide a framework for comparing various qualifications according to their size and level. The qualifications can be indexed like a bookcase, according to Ofqual:

*This framework is your guide to navigating the diverse landscape of qualifications, just like a well-organized bookcase. I**mage copyright: Ofqual. Contains public sector information licensed under the Open Government Licence v3.0.”*

**Characteristics of the qualification**

[**Total Qualification Time (TQT)**](https://www.ymcaawards.co.uk/centres/centre-guidance/regulated-qualifications-framework-faqs/)[**,**](https://www.ymcaawards.co.uk/centres/centre-guidance/regulated-qualifications-framework-faqs/) or the amount of time required to complete the qualification’s coursework, determines the qualification’s size. Since there is no defined deadline for completing an RQF, students are free to complete their credentials at their own pace. Previous qualifications had deadlines.

The TQT examines the following:

The number of hours a typical student will spend studying for the qualification or the hours they will spend receiving instruction from a teacher also known as the number of guided learning hours. (Of course, this is only an approximation; everyone learns differently.)

[**Level of qualification**](https://www.ymcaawards.co.uk/centres/centre-guidance/regulated-qualifications-framework-faqs/)There are 8 levels, which are supported by 3 different entry levels. The**[levels](https://www.twinemployment.com/uk/blog/what-is-rqf-the-new-qcf/)** show how advanced the qualification is. The higher the level number is the more complex and higher the qualification is.  
  
This can be compared to existing qualifications:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Level 1** | **GCSEs (grades 3-1, which were previously D-G)** |
| **Level 2** | GCSEs (grades 9-4, which were previously A\*-C) |
| **Level 3** | A Levels (grade A to E) and/or AS Level |
| **Level 4** | Vocational Qualification Level 4, CertHE, HNC |
| **Level 5** | Vocational Qualification Level 5, Foundation Degree, DipHE, HND |
| **Level 6** | Bachelor Degree (with or without Honours) |
| **Level 7** | Master Degree, Postgraduate Certificate and Diploma, PGCE |
| **Level 8** | Doctoral Degree (Doctor of Philosophy, also known as a PhD) |

Every level includes a qualification descriptor that lists the skills and knowledge related to the qualification as well as the subject matter knowledge and comprehension. Furthermore, unlike QCFs or NVQs containing mandatory units or credits, the RQF has no established qualifying requirements. It is the responsibility of individual awarding organizations to establish their qualifications to ensure their validity and conformity with the labor market demands.

## **Are RQF Courses Better Than QCF and NVQ?**

The RQF’s descriptive makeup allows awarding bodies the freedom to develop, update, and replace qualifications as needed. There needs to be more of a focus on strict rules and structure and more focus on outcomes, the goals of the qualification, and promoting creativity. The RQF can be viewed more as a cataloguing tool: much like a bookcase in a library, the RQF categorizes qualifications by their size and their level, but it doesn’t make any judgments about what those books (or in this case, those courses) contain. For this reason, the RQF is a much better choice than the QCF, both for learners and employers and for educators. When you accomplish an RQF through Inspire London College, our committed team will work diligently to secure positions that match your desired qualifications.

Here is a constructive comparison table summarizing the key differences to understand how these three qualifications differ from one another

**Key Differences:**

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Feature** | **NVQ** | **QCF** | **RQF** |
| **Focus** | Workplace competence | Broader range of skills and knowledge | Specific learning outcomes |
| **Structure** | Unit-based | More complex, modular structure | Streamlined, focus on learning outcomes |
| **Flexibility** | Limited | More flexible for learners to combine qualifications | Highest flexibility for credit transfer and combining qualifications |
| **Accreditation** | Occupational standards | Regulatory body-approved awarding organizations | Regulatory body-approved awarding organizations |
| **Examples** | NVQ Level 3 in Business Administration | QCF Level 4 Diploma in Hospitality Management | RQF Level 5 Bachelor’s Degree in Engineering |
| **Timeline** | 1980s – 2008 | 2008 – 2015 | 2015 – present |

## **What does this mean for learners?**

It won’t make too much difference. All qualifications regulated by Ofqual are still assessed and quality assured in the same way, just under a new framework that makes it easier to see where a qualification sits concerning another. In practical terms, the change simply means that qualification titles and awarding bodies will refer to the ‘RQF’ instead of the ‘QCF’ by the end of 2017 (although some still refer to old titles such as ‘NVQ’ and ‘BTEC’, from the previous.

The RQF has made it easier for qualifications to be designed in direct accordance with market needs, essentially providing more relevant qualifications to learners, making them more attractive to employers.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Both NQFs and RQFs aim to enhance transparency and recognition of qualifications. However, NQFs are nationally focused, while RQFs promote regional collaboration and mobility.
NQFs provide established standards and credibility, but may lack flexibility. RQFs offer adaptability to local needs, but many are still in developmental stages, limiting their effectiveness.

Both frameworks influence learners' access to education and employment opportunities. NQFs can enhance international recognition, whereas RQFs enable regional mobility and skill alignment with local job markets.
